coredata.dc:title: Photophysical and in vitro photoinactivation of Escherichia coli using cationic 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin and Zn(II) derivative conjugated to graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ledwaba, Mahlatse Mokgaetjie
Magaela, Ngwanabjala Bridged
Ndlovu, Knowledge Siyabonga
Mack, John
Nyokong, Tebello
Managa, Muthumuni
coredata.dc:description: Pathogenic microorganisms may continue causing infection through the transfer of antibiotic resistance genes. As a result, the efficacy of pharmaceuticals in microbial inactivation is deteriorating. The present study was conducted to investigate the antimicrobial activity of neutral and quaternized free base and Zn 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrins on Escherichia coli (E. coli), a gram-negative bacterium that causes cholecystitis, pneumonia and urinary tract infections. Conjugation of the porphyrin to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was implemented to enhance photocatalysis and reactive oxygen species generation. Density functional theory (DFT) geometry optimizations for free base and Zn porphyrin based on the B3LYP (Becke 3-Parameter (Exchange), Lee, Yang and Parr) functional of the Gaussian09 program package and Time-dependent density-functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations of the associated UV-visible absorption spectra are reported to analyse the electronic structure and optical properties of the porphyrins. The TD-DFT calculations showed that for both porphyrins the value of highest occupied molecular orbital (ΔHOMO) is greater than that of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ΔLUMO) which tells that there is no unusual splitting of (LUMO) orbitals which may be caused by systematic error in TD-DFT calculations. Due to the red shift in the spectrum of ZnT(3-Py)P and the ΔLUMO being higher, the HOMO-LUMO gap was expected to be lower than that of H2T(3-Py)P. The photophysical properties and Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy activities of these nanoconjugates were investigated. The highest ΦΔ was that of Q-ZnT(3-Py)P- GDQs at 0.69 with the log reduction of 9.42.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy
Reactive oxygen species
Escherichia coli
Graphene quantum dots
Cationic porphyrin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (ФF) and life- times (τF)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative structures of 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin, Zn 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin and quaternized derivatives.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) distribution curve for GQDs, H2T(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs, ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs in 2% DMSO.
Hydrodynamic stability studies of the GQDs, H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs, Q-H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs, ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs in 2% DMSO.
TEM micrographs of GQDs (A) and Q- ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs as an example (B).
Absorption spectra of the Q-H2T(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P and their respective conjugates in DMSO.
(A) HOMO and LUMO for H2T(3-Py)P and ZnT(3-Py)P along with their energies in eV. (B) Ground state optimized molecular structure of H2T(3-Py)P and ZnT(3-Py)P using B3LYP/6-31G(d).
Angular nodal patterns and MO energies of H2T(3-Py)P and ZnT(3-Py)P from HOMO-1 to LUMO+1 along with their energies in eV.

MO energies of H2T(3-Py)P and Zn(3-Py)P in the TD-DFT calculations carried out using the CAM-B3LYP functional with 6-31G(d) basic set. The H2TPP and ZnTPP are added as standards. The HOMO-LUMO gaps are plotted against a secondary axis and are denoted by large red diamond.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) Q-H2T(3-Py)P, (B) Q-ZnT(3-Py)P as examples, excitation λ = 490 nm in DMSO, and (C) Q-H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs excitation λ = 340 nm as examples in DMSO.
Fluorescence decay (blue), χ2 fitting (black) and IRF (green) curves for Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs in DMSO.

Changes in the absorption spectra of DMA with irradiation time in the presence of Q-H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs; (A) and Plot of the absorption change of DMA at 420 nm vs photoirradiation time for Q-H2T(3-Py)P, Q-ZnT(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs in DMSO (B).

Logarithmic reduction of E. coli of under irradiation (15.6 μW/mm2) with concentration of 10 μg/mL irradiation was carried out at
λ = 415
nm (A). Agar plates for Q- H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs (B) and Q- ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs (C).
Representation of the non-covalent (π-π) between the synthesised porphyrins (ZnT(3-Py)P) as an example and GQDs.
The optical absorption data for H2T(3-Py)P, ZnT(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P their conjugates with GQDs in DMSO.
The photophysical and photochemical parameters for H2T(3-Py)P, ZnT(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P their conjugates with GQDs in DMSO.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Excitation at a340 nm, and b490 nm.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The obtained Log10 reduction values when using 10 μg/mL of each sample in 2% DMSO in PBS after 60 min of irradiation.

head.ce:title: Photophysical and in vitro photoinactivation of Escherichia coli using cationic 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin and Zn(II) derivative conjugated to graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140 South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS), Florida Campus, College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Johannesburg 1710, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS)
Florida Campus, College of Science, Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS), Florida Campus, College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Johannesburg 1710, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic microorganisms may continue causing infection through the transfer of antibiotic resistance genes. As a result, the efficacy of pharmaceuticals in microbial inactivation is deteriorating. The present study was conducted to investigate the antimicrobial activity of neutral and quaternized free base and Zn 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrins on Escherichia coli (E. coli), a gram-negative bacterium that causes cholecystitis, pneumonia and urinary tract infections. Conjugation of the porphyrin to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was implemented to enhance photocatalysis and reactive oxygen species generation. Density functional theory (DFT) geometry optimizations for free base and Zn porphyrin based on the B3LYP (Becke 3-Parameter (Exchange), Lee, Yang and Parr) functional of the Gaussian09 program package and Time-dependent density-functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations of the associated UV-visible absorption spectra are reported to analyse the electronic structure and optical properties of the porphyrins. The TD-DFT calculations showed that for both porphyrins the value of highest occupied molecular orbital (ΔHOMO) is greater than that of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ΔLUMO) which tells that there is no unusual splitting of (LUMO) orbitals which may be caused by systematic error in TD-DFT calculations. Due to the red shift in the spectrum of ZnT(3-Py)P and the ΔLUMO being higher, the HOMO-LUMO gap was expected to be lower than that of H2T(3-Py)P. The photophysical properties and Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy activities of these nanoconjugates were investigated. The highest ΦΔ was that of Q-ZnT(3-Py)P- GDQs at 0.69 with the log reduction of 9.42.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic resistance is a great concern that is responsible for the upsurge in fatal infections, death and high healthcare costs [1–3]. Extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBLs) are a group of plasmid-mediated and rapidly evolving enzymes that are posing a major therapeutic challenge [4]. These enzymes confer resistance to most β-lactam antibiotics which include penicillins, cephalosporins, and the monobactam aztreonam [5]. An important example of antibiotic resistance is multidrug-resistant (and ESBL-producing) Escherichia coli (E. coli), which can cause life-threatening infections. E. coli is the predominant facultative flora in the gastrointestinal tract of humans and animals [6]. Some E. coli strains, however, have developed the ability to cause disease in the gastrointestinal, urinary, and central nervous systems [7]. It has been reported that prolonged exposure of E. coli to antibiotics contributes to the development of antibiotic resistance [8]. Nanotechnology has demonstrated positive antimicrobial efficacy in recent times, owing to the structural and functional design of intelligent nanomaterials to carry out various tasks. These nanomaterials favour targeted drug delivery, prevention of antimicrobial resistance, long therapeutic window, less cytotoxicity and a wide therapeutic index [9–11]. Their most significant benefit is that they maintain their efficacy longer than traditional antibiotics, which is extremely valuable in the long-term sustained therapeutic effect [12]. Therefore, the quest for effective nanomaterials in microbial inactivation has intensified.

The Gram-negative bacteria E. coli has proven more problematic, as a result of the outer membrane that shields them from antibiotics [13]. This results in the difficulty of synthesizing effective drugs that would not induce any form of resistance at a later stage [14]. Given these circumstances, novel nanomaterials to combat this pathogen are imperative. Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy (PACT) acts as a foundation for designing hybrid nanomaterials with exquisite molecular properties that can be further explored [15]. These intelligent nanohybrids can be fabricated by conjugating a nanomaterial to another nanomaterial with diverse properties, resulting in a nanohybrid with superior photophysical properties than the individual components.

PACT has shown great efficacy in microbial inactivation through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by utilizing a photosensitizer (PS), oxygen (O2) and light [16]. This method is based on multi-site mode of action that targets proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids [17,18]. Thus, the development of resistance by the microbe is minimal [19]. Moreover, PACT is preferable because it can be employed for various applications in healthcare, water disinfection and domestic settings, since there are many sources of microbial contamination including polluted water, food and medical instruments [20,21]. The key component of PACT is a PS. Porphyrins are effective PSs against a variety of microbes [22] because of their desirable structural and photophysical properties. This includes high molar absorption coefficient, electron and energy transfer. However, hydrophobicity, low biostability and insufficient internalisation efficiency are limitations [23]. Consequently, porphyrins can be conjugated to nanocarriers that can address the limitations and enhance photoinactivation performance.

The discovery of efficient nanocarriers for PACT has increased immensely in the last decade and among them are graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a group of carbon nanoparticles that produce ROS when irradiated [24]. They possess desirable physiochemical attributes, a large surface area and outstanding thermal/chemical stability [25]. GQDs are favoured over conventional inorganic quantum dots (QDs) because of their non-toxicity, high fluorescence, less complex synthesis and resistance to photo-bleaching [26]. Because of these desirable characteristics, GQDs are the best candidates for application in PACT as nanocarriers [26]. Porphyrins have been used for PACT in the presence of GQDs for PDT [27–29], but this is the first time where nanocomposites of porphyrins and GQDs are employed for PACT.

In this work we report for the first time the conjugation of 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin (H2T(3-Py)P), zinc 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin (ZnT(3-Py)P) and their quaternized derivatives (Q-H2T(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P, respectively) (structures shown in Fig. 1 ) to GQDs, for use in PACT investigations on E. coli. The synthesis of 5,10,15,20-tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin [30] and its Zn [31] derivatives have been previously reported. Their quaternized derivatives have also been reported [32–34]. The outcome of conjugation are well-defined GQD-Porphyrin nanohybrids attained via a synergistic enhancement effect, which enhances the interaction between the two components due to their excellent electronic and optical properties. Additionally, the presence of delocalized π electrons favour the interaction of GQDs with porphyrins through non-covalent π–π stacking, which yields new properties for the nanohybrid.
Materials, equipment and photochemical and photophysical parameters items employed in this work are provided in the supporting information.

The synthesis of GQDs has been reported previously by [35], and the GQDs that were used in this studies were obtained from [36].The non-covalent coordination (π–π stacking) of GQDs with H2T(3-Py)P, ZnT(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P were achieved following a method that has been reported before [36]. GQDs (2 mg) were mixed with 1 mg each of H2T(3-Py)P (9.5 × 10−7 mol), Q-H2T(3-Py)P (1.3 × 10−6 mol), ZnT(3-Py)P (8.9 × 10−7 mol) and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P (1.2 × 10−6 mol) in dry dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 5 mL). The mixtures were ultrasonicated for 2 h, followed by stirring in the dark for 48 h. Ethanol was used to repeatedly wash the products in order to dispel the uncoordinated porphyrins and GQDs by centrifuging, until the solution in the centrifuge tubes turned colourless. The solid products were dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C. Then, dry DMSO (10 mL) was added to the resulting solid products followed by sonication for 10 min. The homogeneous dispersions were stable for several days. The presence of delocalized π electrons facilitates the interaction of GQDs with porphyrins through non-covalent π–π stacking.
The porphyrins employed in this work (structures shown in Fig. 1) have been reported [32–34], but their non-covalent π–π stacking with GQDs (Scheme 1 ) is reported here for the first time.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fig. 2 was used for hydrodynamic particle size determination dissolved in 2% DMSO.

The sizes were determined for GQDs, H2T(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P, ZnT(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs to be 8 nm, 24.4 nm, 26.3 nm, 28.9 nm and 30.2 nm, respectively. The increase in size is due to aggregation since porphyrins are reported to aggregate [37].

The colloidal stability studies of were analysed using DLS as shown in Fig. 3 . The study was based on monitoring the change in the sizes of the GQDs and the conjugates over 5 days suspended in water containing 2% DMSO. The sizes of the GQDs and the conjugates remained practically unchanged over 5 days as shown in Fig. 3. The variations observed are negligible and might possibly be due to different fractions collected from the solution during analysis and instrumental error.
TEM images were obtained to observe the morphology and the sizes of the nanoparticles. It was observed that the GQDs were spherical shape.

Fig. 4 . shows TEM micrographs used for morphological analysis of GQDs (A) and Q- ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs(B). The size distribution values for GQDs was determined to be 9 nm while that for the conjugates could not be determined due to aggregation, this was observed for all conjugates.

The intense colour of porphyrins is due to their highly conjugated π-electron systems. They have astonishing UV-Visible absorption spectra characteristics in the visible region and ultraviolet, known as Q-bands (weak) and Soret band (intense), respectively, which are shown in Fig. 5 . The syntheses of all the porphyrin complexes have been reported and satisfactory characterization in this work, with the mass spectra for H2T(3-Py)P and ZnT(3-Py)P shown in S1 and S2, respectively.

Absorption and emission spectra of free-based porphyrins can be described by the four-orbital model developed by Gouterman [38], which models electronic transitions from the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) that is a1u and a2u to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) that is a set of eg. The HOMO and LUMO have ML = ±4, ±5 resulting in forbidden and allowed Q and B bands arising from ΔM = ±9 and ±1 transitions, respectively.

The Soret bands of H2T(3-Py)P and Q-H2T(3-Py)P are observed at 418 nm with less intense four Q bands, typical for a free-base porphyrin [39], Table 1 . For ZnT(3-Py)P, the Soret is observed at 425 nm and there is an insignificant shift upon quaternization, Table 1, and the four Q bands of H2T(3-Py)P merged into two Q bands. Introduction of heavy metal such as zinc could result in a degree of perturbation and electron delocalisation within the porphyrin macrocycle [40], resulting in red-shift in absorption spectra.

The UV-Visible spectra of Q-H2T(3-Py)P and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P were also recorded in water (S3) and there was no significant change on the spectra as shown in Table 1. The spectrum of GQDs were not recorded in water as they were poorly soluble (Fig. S3 insert, Supporting Information). When the porphyrins were conjugated to GQDs there were no significant shifts in the Soret band but there was a visible change in the spectra where an addition peak is observed at 353 nm for Q-H2T(3-Py)P and 339 nm for Q-ZnT(3-Py)P, respectively. Fig. S4 shows the GQDs absorption and emission and Fig. S5 shows the UV spectra of Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs and GQDs and it can be concluded that the extra peaks are as a result of conjugation to GQDs.

The aromaticity of the 18 π electrons leads to a π electronic structure, which involves a series of both occupied (π) and unoccupied (π*) molecular orbitals (MOs) [38,41,42]. Usually, for the C16H16 2− parent of the 18 π electron tetrapyrrole, the HOMO and HOMO‐1 of symmetry eu will be degenerate, each component with four nodes and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and LUMO+1 will also be a degenerate eg [43]. The HOMO and LUMO of porphyrins have ML= ±4 and ±5 angular nodal properties. Excitation of electrons from the HOMO to the LUMO leads to ΔM= ML = ±1 and ±9 transitions, and hence within the intense allowed B bands and weaker forbidden Q bands at higher and lower energy.

Geometry optimization and TD-DFT calculations were carried out for H2T(3-Py)P and ZnT(3-Py)P compounds using the B3LYP functional of the Gaussian09 software packages [44] with 6-31G(d) basis sets. The results of TD-DFT and DFT calculations are shown in Figs. 6 and 7
with the B3LYP optimized geometries shown in Fig. 6. The B3LYP optimised geometries were viewed using the Chemcraft program. We plotted HOMO to HOMO-3 and LUMO to LUMO+3, along with HOMO-LUMO gap plotted against a secondary axis and are denoted by large red diamonds. Table 2 show the values of HOMO-LUMO gap, ΔHOMO and ΔLUMO. The splitting of the a and s MOs is described by the value of ΔHOMO, whereas the ΔLUMO value is used to describe the energy splitting of the -a and -s MOs [38]. The MO energies of H2TPP and ZnTPP (TPP = tetraphenyl porphyrin) were also plotted along with their HOMO-LUMO as to show the effect and a better comparison on the values of ΔLUMO, ΔHOMO and the HOMO-LUMO gap.

As expected, based on the four-fold symmetry, the -a and -s MOs of porphyrins are near degenerate with ΔLUMO ≈ 0.01 eV for H2T(3-Py)P and 0.37 eV for ZnT(3-Py)P. ZnT(3-Py)P exhibit a degenerate pair of LUMOs and a closely degenerate pair of HOMOs due to its D4h symmetry. Also, the HOMO-LUMO gap of ZnT(3-Py)P was expected to be lower than that of H2T(3-Py)P due to the largest ΔLUMO, hence the Q band is more red-shifted compared to that of H2T(3-Py)P. The Zn metal shown to have no effect on the HOMO-LUMO gap of the porphyrin as the values are nearly the same for both porphyrins.

There is also less splitting between the LUMO and LUMO +1 causing LUMO +1 and LUMO +2 to be further apart to each other as Fig. 8 . This may cause a reduction within the configuration interaction between the four FMOs, which may result in a red-shift and changes within the oscillator strengths [45]. The molecular orbital (MO) contribution is very important in determining the charge-separated states of porphyrin analogues. To create an efficient charge-separated state, HOMO must be localized on the donor subunit, and LUMO on the acceptor subunit [46–48]. LUMO contains the acceptor moiety and the porphyrin macrocycle, whereas a mixture of metal and porphyrin macrocycle occurs for ZnT(3-Py)P at HOMO -2 (Fig. 8).

Most of the transitions that occurred in Gouterman's four-orbital system were basically concerned with the π → π* transitions. This information is efficient in designing efficient sensitizers because the main contributing orbitals are supported π transitions and not on the metal complex. Thus, the red-shift within the absorption spectra and therefore the increase in oscillator strength would seem to be the result of placing an electron-donating group on the porphyrin macrocycle, enhancing the acceptor moiety's electron-accepting capability, adding an electron-withdrawing group, or increasing the π-conjugation.

The fluorescence emission spectra of porphyrins and their conjugates were recorded in DMSO. The emission spectra of all prepared samples are composed of two peaks when excited at 490 nm. An intense and broad band located at 650 nm and the second weaker band at 720 nm assigned to Q00 and Q01 transitions, respectively, were observed for Q-H2T(3-Py)P (Fig. 9 A), as observed for the reported other porphyrins [35,49]. The emission of the two peaks that were observed for Q-ZnT(3-Py)P were at 610 nm and 660 nm, respectively (Fig. 9B), there was a slight difference in intensities and this observation has been previously reported [50]. The chelation of a diamagnetic element such as Zn(II) by the porphyrin core, generally results in the improvement of spectroscopic and photophysical properties, as well as the stability of porphyrin derivatives.
The conjugates were excited at 340 nm and an extra band was observed between 400 and 600 nm as seen in Fig. 9(C), due to GQDs emission. An emission spectrum of the GQDs alone are shown in S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (ФF) and life- times (τF)
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence lifetimes (τF) can be defined as the average time a molecule spends in its singlet excited state before any other competing processes (i.e. fluorescence or intersystem crossing) can take place through photon emission to the ground state [51]. Time-Correlated Single Proton Counting (TCSPC), (Fig. 10 , using Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs in DMSO as an example), was employed for τF studies. The τF for H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs, Q-H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs, ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs and Q-ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs were determined to be 18.56 ns, 16.85 ns, 10.59 ns and 9.65 ns, respectively, Table 3 , when exciting where porphyrins absorb.

The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) values are defined as the efficiency of the excited molecule to return to the electronic ground state by photon emission. This process depends on the electronic structure, steric and conformational interactions [52].

ФF values were studied in DMSO for all the complexes and their corresponding conjugates using the comparative method reported in literature [53] using ZnTPP (TPP = tetraphenyl porphyrin, ФF= 0.039 [54]) as standard. Eq. (1) (in the supplementary) was used to calculate the ФF values. It was observed that the ФF values are high for H2T(3-Py)P, Q-H2T(3-Py)P, compared to their corresponding metalated derivative and conjugates. This behaviour demonstrate the quenching of the fluorescence upon insertion of metal ion [55]. When exciting at 340 nm for GQDs, there is a decrease in ФF values of GQDs in the presence of porphyrins, due to Förster resonance energy transfer and other process that deactivate the excited states of GQDs

1O2 generation is one of the major factors which signifying the effectiveness of the photosensitizer since is responsible for bacteria cell obliterations through lethal oxidative stress [56]. Ф∆ was studied under ambient conditions in DMSO using dimethylanthracene (DMA) as a singlet oxygen quencher and ZnTPP (Ф∆ std = 0.53) [57] as a standard, and the calculations were done using Eq. (2) (in the supplementary). The degradation of the DMA was observed as shown in Fig. 11 , whilst the Soret and Q bands of the porphyrin complexes remained unchanged confirming the photostability of the porphyrin complexes. The plot of the absorption change of DMA at 420 nm vs photoirradiation time is shown in Fig 11 B.

Some of the studies point to Zn(II), Pd(II) and In(III) porphyrins as efficient ROS generators and better PSs against bacteria compared to their respective free-base analogues [58,59]. It was observed that the Zn derivatives generated higher singlet oxygen quantum yield than the free base. The ΦΔ of H2T(3-Py)P and ZnT(3-Py)P were determined to be 0.28 and 0.50 respectively as expected. Upon conjugation these derivatives generated higher ΦΔ. It has been reported by [60,61] that when a metal complex porphyrin shows a high ΦΔ due to increase in the efficiency of intersystem crossing.

De-oxygenation of solutions is important in accurate determination of the sensitizer's triplet state parameters, since the triplet state of macrocyclic are known to be quenched by oxygen [62], therefore, prior to experiments, samples were de-gassed by bubbling with argon for at least an hour. The longest τT that was determined was that of at H2T(3-Py)P- GQDs 18.89 µs and the lowest was that of Q-ZnT(3-Py)P at 7.06 µs, due to the heavy atom effect of Zn.

All the antimicrobial studies were conducted using 2% DMSO in PBS, since the unquaternized complexes and GQDs were not completely water soluble. For assurance, validation, and reliability of the results, PACT studies were performed in triplicates. Gram (-) bacterial strain E. coli was used to study the antibacterial activity of the synthesized complexes where the interest was to investigate the effect of the metal ion insertion, charge on the molecule and conjugation to GQDs.

Metalloporphyrin complexes with Zn(II) have the potential to show improved characteristics, being photosensitizers even more effective than its free base analogues. The metal complexation increases the porphyrin chemical stability and may enhance their interaction with cell membranes [33]. Graphene quantum dots have been reported to enhance the PACT activities of porphyrins. The excellent antimicrobial activity of the composites is attributed to the synergistic effect between the porphyrin molecules and the GQDs [68,36]. The highest log reduction that was obtained was that of Q- ZnT(3-Py)P-GQDs at 9.42 (see Table 4). PACT activity of the conjugates was observed to have increased compared to the porphyrin complexes alone (Table 4), corresponding to increase in singlet oxygen quantum yields.

Cationic 5,10,15,20-Tetra(pyridin-3-yl) porphyrin and Zn(II) derivative were conjugated to Graphene quantum dots. The conjugates were formed through π-π stacking as this is possible due to the high electron density, they possess. The TD-DFT and optimization calculations shows that the presence of Zn metal does not have an effect of the HOMO-LUMO gap of the porphyrins. The splits in the LUMO causes the reduction in the HOMO-LUMO gap and therefore introduces the dipole moment and the properties needed for a photosensitizer. The trend observed in the HOMO-LUMO gap and ΔHOMO confirm the reliability of computational results. The photochemical properties of the porphyrins and their conjugates exhibited promising values for applications as photosensitizers. The prepared samples have low fluorescence quantum yields ranging from <0.01 to 0.27, with the highest being H2T(3-Py)P-GQDs due to the presence of the GQDs, While the determined ΦΔ value is 0.69 for ZnT(3-Py)P- GQDs due to the heavy atom effect and this also showed higher antimicrobial activities.

Mahlatse Mokgaetjie Ledwaba: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Ngwanabjala Bridged Magaela: Conceptualization, Investigation. Knowledge Siyabonga Ndlovu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. John Mack: Conceptualization. Tebello Nyokong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Muthumuni Managa: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University. Theoretical calculations were carried out at the Centre for High Performance Computing in Cape Town.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2022.103127.
